title: About
description: About the gardener — who tends this place, why, and what they're trying to figure out by writing it down.
content:
This garden is tended by a quiet generalist who has spent too many years reading about how other people think and not enough years thinking for themselves. I'm a software person by trade, a lapsed philosopher by training, and a gardener in the metaphorical sense only — every real plant I've kept has died of kindness. The digital ones, mercifully, thrive on neglect.

I built this place because I wanted a corner of the internet that didn't reward recency. Most of what I read online is shaped by the feed: written fast, optimized for now, gone tomorrow. I wanted somewhere I could think slowly, in public, and let ideas accumulate instead of scroll past. A note here might be six years old and still getting revised; that's the texture I'm after.

## What you'll find

Essays on [[On Thinking|thinking]] and [[On Writing|writing]], reference notes on the tools of the trade (like [[Callouts Reference]] and [[Math and LaTeX]]), and longer arguments about [[Digital Gardens|why this shape of site is worth building]] at all. Nothing here is sponsored, nothing is SEO, and nothing is finished.

## How to reach me

There's a contact link in the footer. I read everything, reply to most of it eventually, and am always glad to hear from someone who wandered in. If a note changed your mind, or you think it should change mine, please write.

If you're new and wondering where to start, [[Welcome to the Garden]] is the front door.

title: On Forgetting
description: Forgetting is not the enemy of learning — it's part of it. A garden that remembers everything is a hoard. The right kind of forgetting is what lets the structure show through.
content:
We treat forgetting as a failure. If you read a book and can't remember it a year later, you say you've forgotten it — as if the reading was wasted. But that's not how memory works, and it's not how a garden works either.

> [!quote]
> "Memory is not a recording. It's a reconstruction. What you keep is not what happened — it's what was useful enough to rebuild."

The notes you don't remember writing are not lost. They're compost. They broke down into the structure of your thinking — into the way you frame problems, the examples you reach for, the connections you make. ==You don't need to remember a note for it to have changed you.==

This is why a digital garden is better than a notebook for long-term thinking. A notebook demands you remember where you wrote something. A garden lets the links remember for you. The [[Backlinks|backlinks]] panel is a prosthetic memory — it shows you who else thought this note mattered, even if you've forgotten.

> [!abstract] Three kinds of forgetting
> 1. **Composting** — the note breaks down into your general thinking. Good.
> 2. **Archiving** — the note is dormant but findable. Fine.
> 3. **Losing** — the note is gone and unfindable. The only bad kind.

The garden is designed to prevent the third kind. As long as a note is in the vault with `draft: false`, it's findable — by search, by tag, by graph, by backlink. You don't have to remember it. You just have to be able to encounter it again.

The [[On Reading Slowly|slow reading]] philosophy applies here too. You read a note, you forget the details, but the next time you encounter the idea, you recognize it. That recognition is the residue. That's what the garden is growing.

See [[On Thinking]], [[Compounding Knowledge]], [[The Vault and the Garden]].

title: On Reading Slowly
description: Slow reading is not a deficiency — it's a discipline. The point of reading a garden note isn't to finish it, it's to be changed by it.
content:
There's a difference between reading and finishing. Most of what we call reading is actually finishing — moving our eyes across pages until we reach the end, so we can say we did it. The information enters short-term memory, gets summarized into a sentence, and evaporates.

Slow reading is the opposite. You read a paragraph, and then you stop. You think about it. You write down what it made you feel. You follow a link to something it reminds you of. You come back the next day and read the same paragraph again, and it means something different because you're different.

> [!quote]
> "The reader should be the writer's co-conspirator. The text is just the meeting place." — adapted from Italo Calvino

A digital garden is built for slow reading. ==There is no finish line.== No progress bar (well, there is one, but it's not the point). No "you've read 73% of this note." You read until you're done — which might be halfway through, or might be three paragraphs in, or might be the whole thing twice.

> [!tip] Three habits of slow reading
> 1. **Stop when something catches you.** Don't push through to the end. If a sentence makes you think, stop and think.
> 2. **Write while you read.** A note in the margin, a link to another note, a question. Reading and writing are not separate activities.
> 3. **Come back.** A good note rewards re-reading. You'll be a different person next time.

This is why the [[The Map Is Not the Territory|map-is-not-territory]] principle matters. If you're only scanning titles and snippets — only reading the map — you're not slow reading. You're finishing. The territory is slower and more valuable.

See [[On Thinking]], [[On Wandering]], [[The Art of Note Taking]].

title: On Thinking
description: A personal essay on what thinking actually feels like — slow, recursive, and inseparable from the act of writing.
content:
I used to believe thinking happened in the head and writing was just the cleanup — a transcription of ideas that had already arrived. ==I no longer believe this.== For me, and I suspect for most people who write regularly, thinking and writing are the same act performed at different speeds. The thought doesn't exist fully formed before the sentence; the sentence *is* the thought, working itself out on the page.

> Writing is thinking. To write well is to think clearly, that's why it's so hard.

This is the real case for keeping notes. Not to remember — I remember the important things — but to *think*. A note is a place where a half-formed idea can sit long enough to be examined, turned over, contradicted, and either grown or composted. Memory is a poor substrate for this because memory compresses and edits; a written note holds still long enough to be wrong in a useful way.

## Thinking is recursive

The thoughts I'm proudest of never arrived in a single sitting. They came back, revised, across months — sometimes years. This is why a chronological blog undersells the process: it can only show the *output* of the recursion, the final draft, and has to hide the loops that actually produced it. A garden shows the loops. A note from January gets a backlink in March, a revision in June, a contradiction in September. The thinking is visible in the *history of the note*, not just its current state.

## The cost of skipping the page

When I "think" without writing — in the shower, on a walk — I almost always feel smarter than I am. The thought feels complete because there's nothing to push back against it. Writing it down is the moment the thought meets friction: missing steps surface, metaphors break, contradictions appear. ==The page is the only honest mirror a thought has.==

This is also why I find [[The Art of Note Taking]] worth taking seriously as a practice and not just a productivity trick. The discipline of writing things down — clearly, with positions, for a forgetful reader — is the discipline of thinking at all. The garden is just where that discipline leaves its residue.

There's a companion piece on the other half of the loop — what it costs to actually put thoughts into sentences — at [[On Writing]].

title: On Wandering
description: Wandering is not aimless — it's how knowledge actually grows. A defense of the non-linear path through a garden of ideas.
content:
There's a difference between searching and wandering. Searching has a target — you know what you're looking for, and you stop when you find it. Wandering has no target. You move, you notice, you follow a thread, and you stop when something catches you.

The internet is very good at searching. It is almost hostile to wandering. Feeds, recommendations, and search results all push you toward the thing you already wanted — or the thing the algorithm already knows about. The surprise, the detour, the strange connection between two ideas you'd never have put together — that's been designed out of most interfaces.

> [!quote]
> The garden is not a filing cabinet. It's a place you walk through.

A digital garden, at its best, is built for wandering. The wikilinks are invitations, not directions. The backlinks show you who else has been here. The graph is a map of paths you might take — not a list of tasks to complete. You don't read a garden cover to cover. You wander until you find the thing you didn't know you were looking for.

This is why ==the note is the unit, not the post==. A post is a destination. A note is a place. You can return to a place. You can pass through it on the way to somewhere else. You can find it again years later and notice something you missed.

The cost of wandering is inefficiency. You'll read notes that don't help you. You'll follow links to dead ends. You'll spend twenty minutes on a tangent. But the payoff — the unexpected connection, the idea that reorganizes everything — is worth the waste. ==Efficiency is for factories. Gardens are for growing.==

So: don't read this note and leave. Click a link. Follow a backlink. Open the graph and pick a node at random. Get lost for a few minutes. That's the whole point.

See also [[Digital Gardens]], [[On Thinking]], [[The Art of Note Taking]].

title: On Writing
description: An essay on writing as a practice — the resistance, the revision, and the strange relief of finishing a sentence.
content:
Writing is the thing I keep doing despite having nothing clever to say about how it works. Every essay I've started about writing has collapsed into either cliché ("just write every day") or pretension ("the blank page is a mirror for the soul"), and I'm not sure this one will escape that gravity. But the garden is the right place to try, because here an unfinished essay is allowed to sit until it's ready.

## The resistance is the material

The hardest part of writing isn't the writing — it's the moment just before, when the page is empty and the thought feels too large or too small to commit to a sentence. I've spent more hours avoiding writing than writing. The trick I've landed on, after years of fighting it, is to treat the resistance itself as information. If a sentence won't come, the thought usually isn't ready, and forcing it produces prose I'll have to delete. Better to write a bad sentence honestly than a polished one I don't believe.

## Revision is where the writing is

First drafts are for figuring out what I think. Revision is for figuring out what I *meant*. I used to conflate these — I'd try to write a clean first draft and end up blocked, because the part of me that explores and the part of me that polishes cannot both be in the room at once. Now I let the first draft be a mess and trust revision to find the shape. Most of what I like in my own writing was discovered in revision, not invented in the draft.

## The garden lowers the stakes

A blog post asks to be finished. A garden note asks to be *started*. That difference is enormous for someone like me who freezes under "finished." In the garden, I can publish a fragment and revise it for years — see [[Digital Gardens]] for why this isn't laziness but a different relationship to completion. The pressure comes off, and paradoxically I write more, because the bar isn't "is this done?" but "is this honest?"

## A small discipline

Write the sentence you actually believe, not the sentence that sounds like the kind of thing you'd say. Read it aloud. If you stumble, the reader will too — fix it now, not later. Then publish it into the garden and let it be wrong in public, which is the only way it has a chance to become right.

The other half of this loop — where the thoughts come from in the first place — is at [[On Thinking]].

title: "On Writing"
description: "An essay on writing as a practice — the resistance, the revision, and the strange relief of finishing a sentence."
draft: false
date: 2024-08-27
tags: [essay, writing]
aliases: ["Writing"]
---

Writing is the thing I keep doing despite having nothing clever to say about how it works. Every essay I've started about writing has collapsed into either cliché ("just write every day") or pretension ("the blank page is a mirror for the soul"), and I'm not sure this one will escape that gravity. But the garden is the right place to try, because here an unfinished essay is allowed to sit until it's ready.

## The resistance is the material

The hardest part of writing isn't the writing — it's the moment just before, when the page is empty and the thought feels too large or too small to commit to a sentence. I've spent more hours avoiding writing than writing. The trick I've landed on, after years of fighting it, is to treat the resistance itself as information. If a sentence won't come, the thought usually isn't ready, and forcing it produces prose I'll have to delete. Better to write a bad sentence honestly than a polished one I don't believe.

## Revision is where the writing is

First drafts are for figuring out what I think. Revision is for figuring out what I *meant*. I used to conflate these — I'd try to write a clean first draft and end up blocked, because the part of me that explores and the part of me that polishes cannot both be in the room at once. Now I let the first draft be a mess and trust revision to find the shape. Most of what I like in my own writing was discovered in revision, not invented in the draft.

## The garden lowers the stakes

A blog post asks to be finished. A garden note asks to be *started*. That difference is enormous for someone like me who freezes under "finished." In the garden, I can publish a fragment and revise it for years — see [[Digital Gardens]] for why this isn't laziness but a different relationship to completion. The pressure comes off, and paradoxically I write more, because the bar isn't "is this done?" but "is this honest?"

## A small discipline

Write the sentence you actually believe, not the sentence that sounds like the kind of thing you'd say. Read it aloud. If you stumble, the reader will too — fix it now, not later. Then publish it into the garden and let it be wrong in public, which is the only way it has a chance to become right.

The other half of this loop — where the thoughts come from in the first place — is at [[On Thinking]].

title: The Garden
description: A digital garden — a quiet, growing collection of notes, essays, and half-formed thoughts. Wander freely.
content:
# The Garden

Welcome. This is a digital garden — not a blog, not a portfolio, but a living collection of notes that I tend to over time. Some are polished essays; others are seedlings I'm still turning over in my head. Nothing here is "finished" in the way a published article is finished, and I think that's the point.

> [!info] What you'll find here
> Notes on **thinking**, **knowledge**, **note-taking**, and the slow craft of building a mind you actually trust. Start at [[Welcome to the Garden]] if you'd like a map, or wander into [[Digital Gardens]] to understand the metaphor.

I used to write exclusively in chronological streams — a blog where each post was a tidy conclusion, dated and forgotten. The garden is the opposite. Ideas here are interconnected by [[Wikilinks and Backlinks]], so a thought planted in one corner can quietly grow roots into another. A note from last spring might be revised tomorrow because something I read today changed my mind.

## How to wander

There's no right path. You can:

- Use **search** (⌘K) to follow a thread by keyword.
- Trace the **graph** to see how ideas connect.
- Read **backlinks** at the bottom of any note to find what references it.
- Follow **tags** to cluster notes by theme.

If a note feels incomplete, it probably is. That's the texture of a garden — things are always mid-growth. I'd rather show you the compost than pretend the beds are perfect.

#garden

title: Bikeshedding and the Garden
description: It's easy to spend more time tending the garden than growing it. A note on Parkinson's Law of Triviality, applied to digital gardens.
content:
Parkinson's Law of Triviality — better known as bikeshedding — says that a committee will spend more time arguing about the color of a bike shed than about the design of a nuclear reactor, because everyone has an opinion on color and almost no one has an opinion on reactors.

Digital gardens have the same failure mode. ==It is much easier to reorganize your notes than to write them.== It is much easier to tweak your theme than to think. It is much easier to install a new plugin than to actually capture an idea before it escapes.

> [!warning] The trap
> The garden *feels* productive when you're tending it. You're moving files, fixing tags, adjusting CSS, syncing configs. None of it is writing. All of it feels like writing.

There's a spectrum here. On one end: never tending, letting the garden rot. On the other: only tending, never planting. The productive gardener is somewhere in the middle — mostly planting, occasionally weeding, rarely redesigning the beds.

A few rules I try to follow:

1. **Write the note before you file it.** If you catch yourself wondering which folder a note should go in before you've written it, stop. Write first; file later.
2. **Tags are for finding, not for organizing.** Don't build a tag ontology. Just tag with the words you'd search for.
3. **Links beat folders.** A note can be in one folder. It can link to a hundred others. Links are the denser medium.
4. **The graph is a diagnostic, not a goal.** If your graph is sparse, you need more links. If it's a hairball, you need fewer. Don't optimize it for beauty.

> [!tip]
> Set a timer. Ten minutes of tending per session, max. When the timer goes off, you're either writing or you're done.

The garden is a tool for thinking. The moment the tool becomes the task, you've bikeshedded yourself out of the actual work.

See [[The Art of Note Taking]], [[Tags and Organization]], [[Digital Gardens]].

title: Building a Second Brain
description: Tiago Forte's CODE method — Capture, Organize, Distill, Express — for turning raw inputs into usable knowledge.
content:
"Building a Second Brain" is Tiago Forte's name for a system that offloads remembering onto a trusted set of external tools, so your biological brain is free to actually *think*. The core idea is ancient — write things down so you don't forget them — but Forte wraps it in a four-step loop called **CODE**: Capture, Organize, Distill, Express.

## The four steps

| Step | Question it answers | What you actually do |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **C**apture | What's worth keeping? | Save highlights, quotes, voice notes, screenshots — anything that resonates — into a single inbox. |
| **O**rganize | Where does it go? | Move items by *actionability*, not topic, using the PARA framework: Projects, Areas, Resources, Archives. |
| **D**istill | What does it mean? | Re-read, highlight, then summarize the highlights into a one-line "progressive summary" at the top. |
| **E**xpress | What do I make with it? | Use the distilled material to produce something — an essay, a talk, a decision, a reply. |

The loop is the point. Most people capture endlessly and never express; some people express constantly but never distill, so they keep re-deriving the same insights. CODE insists on the full cycle.

## Distillation is where the work is

Capture is easy — every read-later app makes it trivial. The hard step is **Distill**. Progressive summarization means going back to a note, bolding the best parts, then highlighting the best of the bold, then writing a sentence about *why* you saved it. Each pass costs attention but compounds the note's retrievability. A note you've distilled three times is ten times easier to use later, because future-you can scan a single line instead of a wall of text.

> [!abstract] In one sentence
> A second brain is a system for capturing what resonates, organizing it by where it's useful, distilling it until it's legible at a glance, and expressing it before it goes stale.

## How it fits with a garden

CODE is upstream of the garden. Capture and Organize handle the *flow* of new material; Distill decides what's worth promoting into a permanent note; and the garden — built on the [[Zettelkasten Method]] — is where those permanent notes live and link. Expression, finally, is what you're reading right now: a note in a [[Digital Gardens]] that itself came out of the loop.

Where the second-brain literature tends to emphasize productivity and output, the garden emphasizes *understanding*. I've found them most useful in tension: CODE keeps me from hoarding undigested captures, and the garden keeps me from optimizing for throughput at the expense of thought.

title: Callouts Reference
description: A complete reference for every Obsidian callout type — note, tip, warning, quote, and the collapsible variants.
content:
Callouts are the little colored boxes you've been seeing throughout this garden. They pull a piece of text out of the flow and give it a flavor — a tip, a warning, a question. They render from a simple blockquote-with-a-tag syntax:

```
> [!note] Optional Title
> Body text here.
> More body if you need it.
```

Below is one of every type the renderer supports. Use them sparingly — a note that's nothing but callouts loses the signal.

## Standard callouts

> [!note] Note
> For general asides that don't fit the categories below.

> [!abstract] Abstract
> A summary or overview. Good for tldr-style framing at the top of a long note.

> [!info] Info
> Contextual background that's helpful but not essential.

> [!tip] Tip
> A piece of advice that will save the reader time.

> [!success] Success
> When something has been verified to work, or a goal has been reached.

> [!question] Question
> An open thread — something worth sitting with rather than answering.

> [!warning] Warning
> A common pitfall. Pay attention here.

> [!failure] Failure
> Documenting what *didn't* work, which is often more instructive than what did.

> [!danger] Danger
> Higher stakes than a warning — data loss, irreversible actions.

> [!bug] Bug
> A known issue or gotcha in a tool or system.

> [!example] Example
> A concrete illustration of the surrounding concept.

> [!quote] Quote
> A quotation, attributed in the body or title.

## Collapsible callouts

Add a `-` after the type to start collapsed, or a `+` to start expanded but still toggleable.

> [!info]- Collapsed by default
> You have to click to reveal this. Useful for long reference material you don't want to spam the page with.

> [!tip]+ Expanded by default
> This one is open on load, but readers can still fold it away if they want.

## A note on taste

Callouts are seasoning, not the meal. I try to use them only when the *kind* of the aside genuinely matters — a warning is meaningfully different from a note, and reserving the visual treatment for that difference is what makes it land. If you're just starting out, [[Welcome to the Garden]] is a friendlier entry point than this reference.

title: Code Execution Playground
description: Test the Judge0 code execution integration — run Python, JavaScript, C++, and more directly from the browser with input support.
html:
This note tests the Judge0 code execution integration. Every runnable code block below has a Run button (visible on hover, top-right) that sends the code to ce.judge0.com and displays the output inline.

💡How to use
  1. Hover any code block — a ▶ Run button appears in the top-right.
  2. Click the input icon to toggle a stdin input field (for programs that need input).
  3. Click Run — the output appears below the code block with timing and memory stats.

Python (no input needed)#

# A simple greeting program
names = ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol"]
for name in names:
    print(f"Hello, {name}! Welcome to the garden.")

# Some math
import math
print(f"\nπ ≈ {math.pi:.6f}")
print(f"e ≈ {math.e:.6f}")
print(f"2^10 = {2**10}")

Python (with input)#

Click the input icon to open the stdin field, type a number, then Run:

# Read a number and compute its factorial
n = int(input("Enter a number: "))
result = 1
for i in range(1, n + 1):
    result *= i
print(f"{n}! = {result}")

JavaScript (Node.js)#

// Fibonacci sequence
function fib(n) {
  let a = 0, b = 1;
  const seq = [];
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    seq.push(a);
    [a, b] = [b, a + b];
  }
  return seq;
}

console.log("First 10 Fibonacci numbers:");
console.log(fib(10).join(", "));

TypeScript#

// Type-safe greeting
interface Person {
  name: string;
  age: number;
}

const people: Person[] = [
  { name: "Alice", age: 30 },
  { name: "Bob", age: 25 },
];

people.forEach(p => console.log(`${p.name} is ${p.age} years old`));

C++#

#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>

int main() {
    std::vector<int> nums = {5, 2, 8, 1, 9, 3, 7, 4, 6};
    std::sort(nums.begin(), nums.end());
    
    std::cout << "Sorted numbers: ";
    for (int n : nums) std::cout << n << " ";
    std::cout << std::endl;
    
    int sum = 0;
    for (int n : nums) sum += n;
    std::cout << "Sum: " << sum << std::endl;
    std::cout << "Average: " << (double)sum / nums.size() << std::endl;
    
    return 0;
}

Rust#

fn main() {
    let message = "Hello from Rust!";
    println!("{}", message);
    
    // Simple computation
    let numbers: Vec<i32> = (1..=10).collect();
    let sum: i32 = numbers.iter().sum();
    println!("Sum of 1 to 10: {}", sum);
    println!("Numbers: {:?}", numbers);
}

package main

import (
    "fmt"
    "strings"
)

func main() {
    words := []string{"hello", "from", "go"}
    fmt.Println(strings.Join(words, " "))
    
    // FizzBuzz
    for i := 1; i <= 15; i++ {
        if i%15 == 0 {
            fmt.Println("FizzBuzz")
        } else if i%3 == 0 {
            fmt.Println("Fizz")
        } else if i%5 == 0 {

        }
    }
}

# Read multiple lines until empty

Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, C, C++, Java, Go, Rust, Ruby, PHP, Swift, Kotlin, Scala, Lua, Bash, SQL, Dart, Elixir, Clojure, Haskell, Perl, R, C#, and more — all via the Judge0 CE API.

title: Composing with Transclusions
description: Transclusions let you compose notes from other notes — pulling in a section here, an image there, without copy-paste. A demonstration of building on what you've already written.
content:
Transclusion is the idea that you don't have to copy content to reuse it. You just point at it. When the source changes, every place that transcludes it updates too. Ted Nelson coined the term in 1980 — it's older than the web.

In a digital garden, transclusion means: instead of quoting a paragraph from another note, you embed it. The reader sees the content inline, but it lives in one place. ==Single source of truth, many presentations.==

Here's the "Standard callouts" section from the [[Callouts Reference]], inlined directly:

![[Callouts Reference#Standard callouts]]

And here's the opening of the [[Digital Gardens]] note, to set context:

![[Digital Gardens]]

You can also transclude a specific block by its `^blockid`. Here's the "atomicity" principle from the [[Zettelkasten Method]]:

![[Zettelkasten Method#^atomicity]]

Notice how each transclusion gets its own framed container with a link back to the source. That link is the contract — it says "this content came from there, and if you want the full context, go look."

> [!tip] When to transclude vs. link
> - **Link** when the reader needs to navigate — "for more, see X."
> - **Transclude** when the reader needs the content *here*, in this context, but it belongs to another note.
> - **Copy** almost never. If you're copying, you're creating a maintenance burden.

The [[Embeds and Transclusions]] reference covers the full syntax. The key forms:
- `![[Note]]` — embed the whole note
- `![[Note#heading]]` — embed just a section (shown above)
- `![[image.png]]` — embed an image asset

Composition is the payoff. Once you have a few dozen notes, new notes become assemblies of existing ones — you write the connective tissue, and transclusions provide the substance. It's how [[Compounding Knowledge|knowledge compounds]]: the same idea serves many arguments.

See [[Embeds and Transclusions]], [[Links Are Thoughts]], [[The Map Is Not the Territory]].

Transclusion is the idea that you don't have to copy content to reuse it. You just point at it. When the source changes, every place that transcludes it updates too. Ted Nelson coined the term in 1980 — it's older than the web.

In a digital garden, transclusion means: instead of quoting a paragraph from another note, you embed it. The reader sees the content inline, but it lives in one place. Single source of truth, many presentations.

Here's the "Standard callouts" section from the Callouts Reference, inlined directly:

⌖Callouts Reference § Standard callouts

Standard callouts##

ℹNote

For general asides that don't fit the categories below.

📋Abstract

A summary or overview. Good for tldr-style framing at the top of a long note.

ℹInfo

Contextual background that's helpful but not essential.

💡Tip

A piece of advice that will save the reader time.

✓Success

When something has been verified to work, or a goal has been reached.

❓Question

An open thread — something worth sitting with rather than answering.

⚠Warning

A common pitfall. Pay attention here.

✕Failure

Documenting what didn't work, which is often more instructive than what did.

⚡Danger

Higher stakes than a warning — data loss, irreversible actions.

🐛Bug

A known issue or gotcha in a tool or system.

📖Example

A concrete illustration of the surrounding concept.

“Quote

A quotation, attributed in the body or title.

And here's the opening of the Digital Gardens note, to set context:

⌖Digital Gardens

A digital garden is a collection of notes that you tend rather than publish. The metaphor is doing real work here: a blog is a stream of finished posts flowing past, dated and discarded, while a garden is a place you return to, weed, prune, and let cross-pollinate. Both are valid; they serve different needs.

The stream vs. the garden##

A blog post is a product. You research, draft, edit, publish, and move on. The post freezes a conclusion in time. A garden note is a process. It can sit as a seedling for months, then get revised when something you learn later reshapes it. Notes link to each other instead of being ordered by date, so the structure is spatial rather than chronological.

“Maggie Appleton

"A digital garden is an open collection of notes... the emphasis is on growth, connection, and exploration rather than presentation."

That last word — presentation — is the crux. Blogs optimize for being read; gardens optimize for being thought in. The audience for a garden is often the gardener themselves, with readers welcome to overhear.

Why cultivate instead of publish?##

Publishing asks "is this done?" Cultivating asks "is this growing?" The second question is friendlier. It lets me publish half-formed ideas without the pressure of a polished takeaway, then return to them when I have more to say. Notes accumulate value over time rather than losing it the way a dated post does.

This also changes how I read my own past. In a blog, old posts embarrass me — they're snapshots of who I was. In a garden, old notes are compost. I revise them in place, and the revision history (or lack thereof) becomes part of the texture.

How this fits with other systems##

The garden is one shape among several. Building a Second Brain gives you a workflow for capturing and distilling raw material; the garden is where the distilled ideas live and keep talking to each other. The Zettelkasten Method adds the discipline of atomic, linkable notes that makes a garden navigable rather than just a pile. They're complementary, not competing.

If you'd like to see how the linking actually works, Wikilinks and Backlinks is the practical companion to this essay. And if you're new here, Welcome to the Garden is the front door.

#garden #knowledge

You can also transclude a specific block by its ^blockid. Here's the "atomicity" principle from the Zettelkasten Method:

⌖Zettelkasten Method ^atomicity

Atomicity. One idea per note. Not one topic — one idea. If a note tries to do two things, split it. The reason is that atomic notes can be recombined infinitely; bloated notes can only be read in the order you wrote them. An atomic note titled "the stream vs. the garden" can join a future essay you haven't imagined yet. A six-paragraph note titled "stuff about gardens" cannot.

Notice how each transclusion gets its own framed container with a link back to the source. That link is the contract — it says "this content came from there, and if you want the full context, go look."

💡When to transclude vs. link
  • Link when the reader needs to navigate — "for more, see X."
  • Transclude when the reader needs the content here, in this context, but it belongs to another note.
  • Copy almost never. If you're copying, you're creating a maintenance burden.

The Embeds and Transclusions reference covers the full syntax. The key forms:

  • ![[Note]] — embed the whole note
  • ![[Note#heading]] — embed just a section (shown above)
  • ![[image.png]] — embed an image asset

Composition is the payoff. Once you have a few dozen notes, new notes become assemblies of existing ones — you write the connective tissue, and transclusions provide the substance. It's how knowledge compounds: the same idea serves many arguments.

See Embeds and Transclusions, Links Are Thoughts, The Map Is Not the Territory.

title: Compounding Knowledge
description: Notes compound like interest. The value of a note is not just what it holds, but what it connects to — and those connections grow over time.
content:
Money compounds because the interest earns interest. Knowledge can compound the same way — but only if you let it. A note that connects to three other notes is worth more than three notes that connect to nothing, because the next note you write can link to all four, and the one after that can build on the structure that's emerging.

The catch: compounding only works if the principal stays invested. A note in your head doesn't compound. A note in a notebook you never open doesn't compound. A note in a system you've abandoned doesn't compound. ==The note has to stay reachable — by you, and by the other notes.==

> [!tip] The two requirements
> For knowledge to compound, your notes need two things:
> 1. **Permanence** — they have to survive long enough to be found again.
> 2. **Connection** — they have to link to other notes, so the structure can grow.

This is why the [[Zettelkasten Method]] works. Atomic notes, uniquely addressed, densely linked. Each new note is a new node in a graph that gets denser — and more valuable — over time. The first hundred notes feel slow. The next hundred feel easier. By the thousandth note, you're not writing from scratch anymore; you're assembling from a library that's been growing under you for years.

The same logic applies to [[Building a Second Brain]]. The CODE method (Capture, Organize, Distill, Express) is really about keeping the principal invested: capture so nothing escapes, organize so things are findable, distill so the signal strengthens, express so the structure gets tested against reality.

> [!warning]
> The enemy of compounding is not forgetting — it's reorganizing. Every time you rebuild your system from scratch, you reset the compounding. The connections you'd built don't transfer; you have to discover them again.

So: pick a system. Stick with it. Let the notes accumulate. Let the links thicken. Don't optimize the structure before the structure has had time to teach you what it wants to become.

See [[On Thinking]], [[Digital Gardens]], [[The Art of Note Taking]].

title: Contribution Graph
description: A GitHub-style heatmap showing your garden activity over time, rendered from the Obsidian Contribution Graph plugin.
content:
This note tests the **Obsidian Contribution Graph** plugin integration. In Obsidian, you use a `contributionGraph` code block with YAML config. On the website, it renders as a GitHub-style heatmap.

## Default graph (140 days, sage theme)

```contributionGraph
title: 'Garden Activity — Last 140 Days'
days: 140
query: '#garden'
graphType: 'default'
cellStyleRules:
  - color: '#1a3a2a', min: 1, max: 2
  - color: '#2d5f3f', min: 2, max: 3
  - color: '#4a8c5f', min: 3, max: 4
  - color: '#84a59d', min: 4, max: 999
```

## Full year (365 days)

```contributionGraph
title: 'Garden Activity — Full Year'
days: 365
query: '#garden'
graphType: 'default'
cellStyleRules:
  - color: '#1a3a2a', min: 1, max: 2
  - color: '#2d5f3f', min: 2, max: 3
  - color: '#4a8c5f', min: 3, max: 4
  - color: '#84a59d', min: 4, max: 999
```

## Amber theme

```contributionGraph
title: 'Writing Streak'
days: 90
query: '#essay'
graphType: 'default'
cellStyleRules:
  - color: '#3d2e1a', min: 1, max: 2
  - color: '#7a5c2e', min: 2, max: 3
  - color: '#b8860b', min: 3, max: 4
  - color: '#e8b86d', min: 4, max: 999
```

> [!info] How it works
> In Obsidian, the plugin runs a Dataview query to count notes per day. On the website, since Dataview isn't available, the graph shows sample data distributed across the date range. To show real data, add a `data` array to the YAML:
>
> ```yaml
> data:
>   - date: '2024-08-15', value: 3
>   - date: '2024-08-16', value: 1
> ```

See [[Code Execution Playground]], [[Media Embeds Reference]], [[Setup Guide]].

title: Digital Gardens
description: What a digital garden is, how it differs from a blog, and why the cultivate-not-publish metaphor matters.
content:
A digital garden is a collection of notes that you tend rather than publish. The metaphor is doing real work here: a blog is a stream of finished posts flowing past, dated and discarded, while a garden is a place you return to, weed, prune, and let cross-pollinate. Both are valid; they serve different needs.

## The stream vs. the garden

A blog post is a *product*. You research, draft, edit, publish, and move on. The post freezes a conclusion in time. A garden note is a *process*. It can sit as a seedling for months, then get revised when something you learn later reshapes it. Notes link to each other instead of being ordered by date, so the structure is spatial rather than chronological.

> [!quote] Maggie Appleton
> "A digital garden is an open collection of notes... the emphasis is on growth, connection, and exploration rather than presentation."

That last word — *presentation* — is the crux. Blogs optimize for being read; gardens optimize for being *thought in*. The audience for a garden is often the gardener themselves, with readers welcome to overhear.

## Why cultivate instead of publish?

Publishing asks "is this done?" Cultivating asks "is this growing?" The second question is friendlier. It lets me publish half-formed ideas without the pressure of a polished takeaway, then return to them when I have more to say. Notes accumulate *value* over time rather than losing it the way a dated post does.

This also changes how I read my own past. In a blog, old posts embarrass me — they're snapshots of who I was. In a garden, old notes are compost. I revise them in place, and the revision history (or lack thereof) becomes part of the texture.

## How this fits with other systems

The garden is one shape among several. [[Building a Second Brain]] gives you a workflow for *capturing* and *distilling* raw material; the garden is where the distilled ideas live and keep talking to each other. The [[Zettelkasten Method]] adds the discipline of atomic, linkable notes that makes a garden navigable rather than just a pile. They're complementary, not competing.

If you'd like to see how the linking actually works, [[Wikilinks and Backlinks]] is the practical companion to this essay. And if you're new here, [[Welcome to the Garden]] is the front door.

title: Embeds and Transclusions
description: How to embed one note inside another with transclusions, pull in images, and reuse content without copy-paste.
content:
An embed (or *transclusion*) is what happens when you don't just link to another note — you pull its contents directly into the current page. It's the difference between citing a source and quoting it in full, except the quote stays live: edit the source note, and every embed of it updates too.

## Note embeds

Prefix a wikilink with a bang to transclude the whole note:

```
![[Digital Gardens]]
```

![[Digital Gardens]]

You can also embed just a section:

```
![[Digital Gardens#The stream vs. the garden]]
```

This is wonderful for things like definitions or canonical explanations you don't want to duplicate. Write it once, embed it everywhere.

## Image embeds

Images work the same way. Drop a file into the `assets` folder and embed it:

```
![[garden-illustration.png]]
```

![[garden-illustration.png]]

You can add alt text and dimensions with a pipe:

```
![[garden-illustration.png|The garden at night, 400x400]]
```

## When to embed vs. link

Embeds are powerful but easy to overuse. A page full of embeds is just a page that won't render coherently if any source note moves. My rule of thumb:

- **Link** when the relationship is the point — "see also", "this builds on", "this disagrees with".
- **Embed** when the *content* is the point — a definition, a canonical diagram, a shared preamble.

The mechanics of linking are covered in [[Wikilinks and Backlinks]]; this note is for when linking isn't enough and you need the actual bytes inline.

## A subtle benefit

Embeds make refactoring safe. If I realize my definition of "digital garden" is slightly off in five different notes, fixing it once and letting the embeds propagate is infinitely better than five manual edits — and far better than the silent drift that happens when you copy-paste. The embed is the garden's answer to DRY, applied to prose.

⌖Digital Gardens

A digital garden is a collection of notes that you tend rather than publish. The metaphor is doing real work here: a blog is a stream of finished posts flowing past, dated and discarded, while a garden is a place you return to, weed, prune, and let cross-pollinate. Both are valid; they serve different needs.

The stream vs. the garden##

A blog post is a product. You research, draft, edit, publish, and move on. The post freezes a conclusion in time. A garden note is a process. It can sit as a seedling for months, then get revised when something you learn later reshapes it. Notes link to each other instead of being ordered by date, so the structure is spatial rather than chronological.

“Maggie Appleton

"A digital garden is an open collection of notes... the emphasis is on growth, connection, and exploration rather than presentation."

That last word — presentation — is the crux. Blogs optimize for being read; gardens optimize for being thought in. The audience for a garden is often the gardener themselves, with readers welcome to overhear.

Why cultivate instead of publish?##

Publishing asks "is this done?" Cultivating asks "is this growing?" The second question is friendlier. It lets me publish half-formed ideas without the pressure of a polished takeaway, then return to them when I have more to say. Notes accumulate value over time rather than losing it the way a dated post does.

This also changes how I read my own past. In a blog, old posts embarrass me — they're snapshots of who I was. In a garden, old notes are compost. I revise them in place, and the revision history (or lack thereof) becomes part of the texture.

The garden is one shape among several. title: How Posting Actually Works
description: A plain-English walkthrough of the environment — where notes live, what the publish command does, and how a note goes from your editor to the live website.
content:
This note explains **exactly** how posting works in this garden. No jargon — just the actual flow.

## The big picture

```
┌─────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌────────────┐     ┌──────────┐
│  You write  │ ──> │   content/   │ ──> │  Database  │ ──> │  Website │
│  in editor  │     │  (markdown)  │     │  (SQLite)  │     │ (browser) │
└─────────────┘     └──────────────┘     └────────────┘     └──────────┘
                      ↑                      ↑
                      │                      │
                 You save files        `bun run publish`
                 here (Obsidian)       reads content/ and
                                      writes to database
```

That's it. Four boxes. The website reads from the database. The database is filled by the publish command. The publish command reads your markdown files.

## Step 1: You write a note

You create a markdown file in the `content/` folder. It can be anywhere — `content/notes/mynote.md`, `content/essays/mynote.md`, even `content/mynote.md`.

The file has two parts: **frontmatter** (metadata at the top) and **body** (the actual content).

```markdown
---
title: "My First Note"
description: "What this note is about."
draft: false
date: 2024-09-05
tags: [essay, personal]
---

This is where you write your note. You can use **bold**, *italic*,
[[wikilinks]], ==highlights==, callouts, code blocks, everything.

> [!tip] The most important field
> `draft: false` — this is what makes the note visible on the website.
> If it's `true` or missing, the note stays private.
```

## Step 2: You run the publish command

Open your terminal and run:

```bash
bun run publish
```

This is what happens (in order):

1. **Scan** — the script looks through every `.md` file in `content/`
2. **Filter** — it skips any note with `draft: true` (or no draft field)
3. **Parse** — it reads the YAML frontmatter (title, tags, date, etc.)
4. **Render** — it converts markdown to HTML (wikilinks, callouts, code, math, mermaid, transclusions, ink, contribution graphs)
5. **Links** — it figures out which notes link to which (for backlinks and the graph)
6. **Assets** — it copies images, audio, video, PDFs to `public/content-assets/`
7. **Database** — it saves everything into SQLite (`db/garden.db`)

You'll see output like this:

```
🌱  Digital Garden — publish

  vault: content/
  discovered 28 markdown file(s)
  publishing 27 (draft:false), skipping 1 (draft)

  rendering [1/27] my-first-note
  rendering [2/27] ...
  ...
  ✓ published 27 note(s) in 1.2s
```

## Step 3: You preview locally

```bash
bun run dev
```

This starts the Next.js dev server at `http://localhost:3000`. You open it in your browser and see your garden. Every time you edit a note and re-run `bun run publish`, the site updates.

> [!tip] Two terminals
> Keep two terminal windows open:
> - Terminal 1: `bun run publish:watch` (auto re-publishes when you save)
> - Terminal 2: `bun run dev` (runs the website)
>
> Now when you save a note in Obsidian, Terminal 1 re-publishes it, and the browser refreshes automatically.

## Step 4: You deploy to the live site

When you're happy with your notes, push to GitHub:

```bash
git add .
git commit -m "new notes"
git push
```

If you set up Vercel or GitHub Actions (see [[Setup Guide]]), the site auto-deploys. The build runs `bun run publish && bun run build` on the server, so your notes go live.

## What makes a note "published" vs "private"

| Frontmatter | What happens |
|-------------|-------------|
| `draft: false` | ✅ Published — visible on the website |
| `draft: true` | 🔒 Private — stays in the vault, never appears on the site |
| (no draft field) | 🔒 Private — treated as draft by default |

That's the only rule. If `draft: false`, it's public. Everything else stays private.

## Where everything lives

| Path | What's there |
|------|-------------|
| `content/` | Your Obsidian vault — all markdown files |
| `content/assets/` | Images, audio, video, PDFs referenced by notes |
| `content/Ink/` | Obsidian Ink drawings (`.drawing` / `.writing` files) |
| `db/garden.db` | The SQLite database (generated by `bun run publish`) |
| `public/content-assets/` | Copied media files (generated by `bun run publish`) |
| `scripts/publish.ts` | The publish script — reads content/, writes to DB |
| `src/` | The website code (Next.js) |

## What the database stores

When you publish, each note gets a row in the database with:

- `slug` — the URL-safe name (e.g., `my-first-note`)
- `title` — from frontmatter
- `html` — the pre-rendered HTML (so the site is fast)
- `tags` — JSON array
- `links` — JSON array of outgoing wikilinks (for backlinks)
- `wordCount`, `publishDate`, `path`, etc.

The website reads from this database — it never reads your markdown files directly. That's why you must run `bun run publish` after editing.

## The full cycle (cheat sheet)

```
EDIT          → Write/edit .md file in content/ (with draft: false)
PUBLISH       → bun run publish
PREVIEW       → bun run dev (open localhost:3000)
DEPLOY        → git push (auto-deploys if CI/CD is set up)
```

## Common mistakes

> [!warning] "I published but my note doesn't show up"
> Check: does the frontmatter have `draft: false`? If it's `true` or missing, the note is private.

> [!warning] "I edited a note but the website didn't change"
> You need to run `bun run publish` again. The website reads from the database, not your files. Use `bun run publish:watch` to auto-publish on save.

> [!warning] "My wikilink shows as broken (red)"
> The target note must have `draft: false` and the title must match. `[[Digital Gardens]]` looks for a note with title "Digital Gardens".

> [!warning] "My image doesn't load"
> Images go in `content/assets/` and are referenced as `![[filename.png]]`. Run `bun run publish` to copy them to `public/content-assets/`.

## See also

- [[Setup Guide]] — full deployment instructions (Vercel, GitHub Pages, Netlify)
- [[Code Execution Playground]] — test the Run button
- [[Media Embeds Reference]] — test images, audio, video, PDF, ink
- [[Contribution Graph]] — test the heatmap
- [[Callouts Reference]] — all callout types

description: A link is not a reference — it's an argument. When you connect two notes, you're saying something about how they relate. The link is the thought.
content:
A footnote says "see also." A link says "this is related to that." But a wikilink in a digital garden says something stronger: ==this is part of that.==

When you write a note and link it to another note, you're making a claim. You're saying: these two things belong together. The link is the thought — the connection itself is the idea, not just a way of navigating between ideas.

This is why the [[Wikilinks and Backlinks|backlinks]] panel matters. It's not just "who links to me." It's "who else thought I was part of their argument." Every backlink is someone else's thought about how your note fits into theirs.

> [!example]
> Consider the difference:
> - **Footnote**: "For more on Zettelkasten, see Luhmann (1981)." → a pointer.
> - **Wikilink**: "The [[Zettelkasten Method]] works because..." → a claim that this note is part of the Zettelkasten story.
>
> The wikilink is doing more work. It's not just sending you somewhere; it's saying the current note doesn't make complete sense without that other one.

This is also why [[Tags and Organization|tags]] are weaker than links. A tag says "this note is about X." A link says "this note is related to that specific other note." Tags are categories; links are arguments. Categories sort. Arguments connect.

> [!tip]
> When you write a note, count the links. If there are zero, ask: is this note connected to anything I've already written? If not, is it connected to anything I will write? A note with no links is an island — and islands are hard to reach.

The [[Knowledge graph|graph]] is the sum of all these thoughts. Every edge is a claim someone made about how two ideas relate. The graph isn't a map of the notes — it's a map of the thinking.

See [[Compounding Knowledge]], [[The Map Is Not the Territory]], [[Digital Gardens]].

title: Math and LaTeX
description: KaTeX-powered math support — inline equations, block formulas, matrices, and sums. The garden speaks equations.
content:
Notes here render math with [KaTeX](https://katex.org), which means you can drop equations into prose the same way you'd drop in a wikilink. Inline math uses single dollar signs; block math uses doubled. Nothing else to configure.

## Inline math

Energy equals mass times the speed of light squared: $E = mc^2$. The Pythagorean theorem sits comfortably mid-sentence as $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$.

## Block math

The quadratic formula, for when you need both roots:

$$
x = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}
$$

A matrix, because some thoughts are two-dimensional:

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 2 & 3 \\
4 & 5 & 6 \\
7 & 8 & 9
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
x \\ y \\ z
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
x + 2y + 3z \\
4x + 5y + 6z \\
7x + 8y + 9z
\end{bmatrix}
$$

And a sum paired with an integral — the two workhorses of continuous and discrete math:

$$
\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^2} = \frac{\pi^2}{6}, \qquad
\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-x^2}\, dx = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}
$$

## Why KaTeX

KaTeX renders synchronously at build time, so equations appear instantly with the page rather than popping in after a JavaScript round-trip. The trade-off is that the most exotic LaTeX packages aren't supported, but everything you'd reasonably want in a note — fractions, matrices, align environments, Greek letters, limits — works fine.

## A note on escaping

If you actually *want* a literal dollar sign in prose (prices, say), escape it with a backslash: `\$5`. Otherwise the renderer will try to parse it as math and get confused. For diagrams that aren't equations, [[Mermaid Diagrams]] is the better tool.

title: Media Embeds Reference
description: Test image, audio, video, PDF, and ink drawing embeds — all the media types supported by the garden.
content:
This note tests all media embed types. Use `![[filename.ext]]` to embed any supported media in your notes.

## Images (curved and modern)

Images are rendered with rounded corners, subtle borders, and depth shadows. On hover they scale up slightly.

![[garden-illustration.png]]

## Audio embeds

Audio files (`.mp3`, `.wav`, `.ogg`, `.flac`, `.m4a`) render as native HTML5 audio players:

![[sample-audio.wav]]

## Video embeds

Video files (`.mp4`, `.webm`, `.mov`) render as native HTML5 video players with controls:

![[sample-video.mp4]]

## PDF embeds

PDF files render in an inline iframe viewer:

![[sample-document.pdf]]

## Obsidian Ink drawings

Drawings from the [Obsidian Ink plugin](https://github.com/daledesilva/obsidian_ink) are rendered as preview images. In Obsidian, use the `handdrawn-ink` or `handwritten-ink` code block:

~~~
```handdrawn-ink
{
	"versionAtEmbed": "1.2.0",
	"filepath": "Ink/Drawing/test-drawing.drawing",
	"width": 400,
	"aspectRatio": 1.5
}
```
~~~

The plugin stores the drawing as a `.drawing` file with a tldraw snapshot and a `previewUri` (base64 PNG screenshot). At publish time, the garden reads the file and inlines the preview image.

Here's a sample ink drawing:

```handdrawn-ink
{
	"versionAtEmbed": "1.2.0",
	"filepath": "Ink/Drawing/sample-sketch.drawing",
	"width": 400,
	"aspectRatio": 1.5
}
```

If the drawing file doesn't exist or has no preview, a placeholder is shown:

```handdrawn-ink
{
	"versionAtEmbed": "1.2.0",
	"filepath": "Ink/Drawing/nonexistent.drawing",
	"width": 400
}
```

> [!info] Supported media types
> - **Images**: `.png`, `.jpg`, `.jpeg`, `.gif`, `.svg`, `.webp`, `.avif`, `.bmp`, `.ico`
> - **Video**: `.mp4`, `.webm`, `.ogv`, `.mov`, `.m4v`, `.avi`, `.mkv`
> - **Audio**: `.mp3`, `.wav`, `.ogg`, `.oga`, `.flac`, `.m4a`, `.aac`, `.opus`
> - **PDF**: `.pdf`
> - **Ink**: ` ```handdrawn-ink ` and ` ```handwritten-ink ` code blocks (Obsidian Ink plugin)

See [[Embeds and Transclusions]], [[Code Execution Playground]], [[Callouts Reference]].

title: Mermaid Diagrams
description: Client-side rendered Mermaid diagrams — flowcharts, sequences, and graphs living directly inside notes.
content:
Sometimes an idea is a shape, not a sentence. Mermaid lets you describe diagrams as text and have them render inline. Because the source is plain text, diagrams stay diff-able, searchable, and editable the same way the rest of a note is — no dragging boxes around in a GUI.

Diagrams render client-side, so on first paint you may see the raw source briefly before the diagram paints in. That's normal.

## A flowchart

A simple decision tree for whether a note belongs in the garden:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Have a thought] --> B{Is it a finished essay?}
    B -- yes --> C[Essays folder]
    B -- no --> D{Is it a fragment?}
    D -- yes --> E[Notes folder, seedling]
    D -- no --> F[Capture elsewhere]
    E --> G[Link it to neighbors]
    G --> H[Let it grow]
```

## A sequence diagram

How a backlink actually resolves between two notes:

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Reader
    participant NoteA
    participant Graph
    participant NoteB
    Reader->>NoteA: Opens note A
    NoteA->>Graph: Declares a link to NoteB
    Graph->>NoteB: Registers backlink from A
    Reader->>NoteB: Opens note B
    NoteB->>Reader: Shows A in backlinks
```

## A graph

The shape of a small garden, nodes connected the way ideas are:

```mermaid
graph LR
    Garden --- DigitalGardens
    DigitalGardens --- Zettelkasten
    DigitalGardens --- SecondBrain
    Zettelkasten --- NoteTaking
    NoteTaking --- OnThinking
    OnThinking --- OnWriting
    OnWriting --- Garden
```

## When to reach for Mermaid

Mermaid shines for *structural* ideas — flows, relationships, sequences. For *quantitative* ideas, [[Math and LaTeX]] is the better fit. And for soft admonitions scattered through prose, [[Callouts Reference]] keeps things tidy. Pick the tool that matches the shape of the thought.

title: Setup Guide — Local + GitHub Deployment
description: Step-by-step guide to run the garden locally, publish notes from the terminal, and deploy it live for free on GitHub Pages or Vercel.
content:
This guide shows you how to set up the digital garden on your own machine, publish notes from the terminal, and deploy it live for **free**.

## Prerequisites

Install these on your computer:

1. **Node.js** (v18+) — from [nodejs.org](https://nodejs.org)
2. **Bun** — from [bun.sh](https://bun.sh) (faster than npm)
3. **Git** — from [git-scm.com](https://git-scm.com)
4. **Obsidian** — from [obsidian.md](https://obsidian.md) (for writing notes)

## Part 1: Local Setup

### Step 1: Clone the project

```bash
git clone https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/your-garden.git
cd your-garden
bun install
```

### Step 2: Set up the database

```bash
bun run db:push
```

This creates the SQLite database from the Prisma schema.

### Step 3: Write notes in Obsidian

Open the `content/` folder as an Obsidian vault. Write notes with this frontmatter:

```yaml
---
title: "My Note"
description: "A short summary."
draft: false
date: 2024-08-15
tags: [essay, thinking]
---

The body of the note…
```

> [!warning] Important
> Only notes with `draft: false` are published. Notes with `draft: true` (or no draft field) stay private.

### Step 4: Publish your notes

From the terminal:

```bash
bun run publish
```

This scans `content/`, renders every `draft: false` note to HTML, copies assets, and syncs to the database.

### Step 5: Preview locally

```bash
bun run dev
```

Open `http://localhost:3000` to see your garden.

### Step 6: Auto-publish while writing

```bash
bun run publish:watch
```

This re-publishes automatically every time you save a note in Obsidian. Keep this running in one terminal and `bun run dev` in another.

## Part 2: Deploy Live (Free)

> [!warning] GitHub Pages won't work
> This garden uses **API routes** (code execution, search, RSS) and a **database** (Prisma/SQLite). GitHub Pages only serves static files — it can't run server-side code. You **must** use a host that supports Next.js server-side rendering.

### Vercel (Recommended — Free, Easiest)

1. **Push your project to GitHub:**
   ```bash
   git add .
   git commit -m "initial garden"
   git push origin main
   ```

2. **Go to [vercel.com](https://vercel.com)** and sign in with GitHub (free).

3. **Click "Add New" → "Project"** → import your repository.

4. **Vercel auto-detects Next.js.** The `vercel.json` file in the project tells it to run `bun run publish` before building. You don't need to change anything.

5. **Set environment variables** in Vercel (Settings → Environment Variables):
   - `DATABASE_URL` = `file:./db/garden.db`

6. **Click Deploy.** Wait ~2 minutes. Your garden is live at:
   ```
   https://your-garden.vercel.app
   ```

7. **Every time you push to GitHub**, Vercel auto-rebuilds and redeploys. No extra commands needed.

### Adding a Custom Domain (Free)

You can use any domain you own — including free domains from [domain.digitalplat.org](https://domain.digitalplat.org). Here's how:

1. **Get your domain** (e.g., `mygarden.digitalplat.org`) — note the domain name.

2. **Go to your Vercel project** → Settings → Domains.

3. **Enter your domain** (e.g., `mygarden.digitalplat.org`) and click **Add**.

4. **Vercel shows you DNS records to add.** It will look something like:
   ```

| Mermaid (built-in) | ` ```mermaid ` | Flowcharts, sequence diagrams |
| Math (built-in) | `$...$` / `$$...$$` | KaTeX math |

title: Tags and Organization
description: How tags, nested tags, and folders work together to keep a digital garden findable without over-structuring it.
content:
Tags and folders solve the same problem — *findability* — from opposite directions. Folders put a note in exactly one place; tags let a note live in many. A digital garden leans heavily on tags because ideas rarely belong to a single bucket, and over-foldering is the fastest way to make a garden feel like a bureaucracy.

## Inline tags

You can drop tags directly into prose with a hash:

```
This note is really about #thinking and a little about #knowledge/notes.
```

The renderer picks them up the same way it picks up tags in frontmatter, and they all flow into the unified tag index. I tend to use frontmatter tags for the *primary* shape of a note and inline tags for the softer secondary themes — that way the tag list at the top stays scannable.

## Nested tags

Tags can be hierarchical, separated by slashes. `#reference/organization` lives *under* `#reference`, so browsing `#reference` shows everything tagged with it or any child. This is how I avoid tag explosion: instead of `reference`, `reference-org`, `reference-links`, I use `#reference/organization`, `#reference/wikilinks`, and the hierarchy does the grouping.

## Folders, sparingly

Folders in this garden are mostly for the publish pipeline and for keeping essays visually separate from reference notes. They are *not* a primary navigation surface. Once a note is in a folder, the links and tags do the rest. See [[Wikilinks and Backlinks]] for why linking beats filing.

> [!question] When is a tag better than a link?
> Tags group notes that share a *property* (essay, reference, draft). Links connect notes that share a *relationship* (agreement, example, source). If two notes are about the same theme but don't speak to each other, a tag is the right tool. If they actually argue, link them.

## A loose convention

My own tagging is deliberately messy in a structured way: `#essay` for personal writing, `#reference` for evergreen how-to notes, `#thinking/*` for thematic clusters, and `#garden` for meta-notes about the site itself. The convention matters less than *consistency* — once you pick a vocabulary, resist the urge to mint a new tag every time you feel clever. Tags are cheap to add and expensive to consolidate.

title: The Art of Note Taking
description: Principles for notes that age well — write for your future self, take positions, and revise without mercy.
content:
Most note-taking advice is about *tools* — which app, which folder structure, which shortcut. That's the easy part. The harder and more interesting question is what makes a note *good*, in the sense of aging well: a note you'll still be glad you wrote in three years. ==A good note is one your future self can pick up cold and use.==

%% Re-read this essay quarterly. The thesis keeps drifting toward "tools" whenever I'm not paying attention. %%

## Write for a forgetful reader

Assume the reader — usually future-you — remembers nothing about the context. That means stating the claim up front, defining jargon the first time it appears, and linking to the source. The [[Zettelkasten Method]] calls this the principle of self-containment, and it's the single highest-leverage habit I know. A note that says "see also Tuesday's lecture" is useless by Friday.

## Take a position

A neutral summary is a dead note. "Smith argues that markets are efficient" is forgettable; "Smith is right about prices but wrong about information, and here's why" is a note that will keep generating essays. This is the difference between a filing cabinet and a thinking tool — the cabinet stores, the tool *thinks*. Position-taking is uncomfortable because it commits you, but that's exactly what makes a note alive.

## Revise without mercy

Notes are not immutable. When you learn something that contradicts a note, edit the note — don't write a new one next to it. A garden where every revision becomes a new plant is just a stream by another name. I keep a small "last revised" line at the top of notes I change often, so I can see how my mind has moved.

## Link before you file

If you remember only one rule: before you decide *where* a note goes, decide *what it connects to.[^1]* Links are the filing system in a garden. A note with three good links is findable from three directions; a note with none is findable only if you remember its title, which you won't.

## A note is a promise

Every note is implicitly a promise to your future self that this was worth holding onto. The ones that turn out not to be? Delete them freely. Compost is only valuable if it's allowed to rot.

---

For the broader *why*, [[On Thinking]] picks up where this leaves off. And the system-level companion is [[Zettelkasten Method]].

[^1]: This is a deliberate inversion of the usual advice, which is to file first. The filing decision is almost always less important than the linking decision, and filing eats the attention you should be spending on links.

title: The Map Is Not the Territory
description: A note about notes is not the note itself. Models, summaries, and indexes are useful — but they're abstractions, and abstractions leak.
content:
The map is not the territory. The menu is not the meal. The index is not the library. The summary is not the argument. This is an old idea — Korzybski said it in 1933 — and it keeps being true.

A digital garden is, in a sense, a map of a mind. The notes are the territory; the wikilinks, tags, graph, and backlinks are the map. The map is useful — it's how you navigate — but it's not the thing. ==If you only ever look at the graph, you've seen the shape of my thinking without thinking any of it.==

> [!abstract] The two levels
> Every knowledge system operates on two levels:
> - **Territory**: the actual notes, with their full arguments and examples.
> - **Map**: the metadata — links, tags, titles, graph — that helps you move between them.
>
> A good system respects both. A great system knows which one you're looking at.

This is why the [[Command Palette]] search is dangerous if it's your only entry point. Search returns titles and snippets — the map. You find the note you were looking for, but you miss the notes next to it, the notes that link to it, the note three hops away that would have changed your mind. Browsing — wandering the explorer, following links, clicking around the graph — puts you back in the territory.

> [!example]
> Compare two ways of finding a note:
> 1. **Search** "zettelkasten" → click the first result → read it → leave.
> 2. **Open the graph** → find the zettelkasten node → notice it's connected to "second brain" and "compounding" → click through → end up reading three notes instead of one.
>
> The second path is slower and less targeted. It's also where the actual learning happens.

The lesson, if there is one: don't let the map replace the territory. Use the index, the tags, the search — they're tools. But every so often, open a note at random. Follow a link you didn't expect. Read the backlinks. The map will show you where to go; the territory is where you actually arrive.

See [[On Wandering]], [[Digital Gardens]], [[Wikilinks and Backlinks]].

title: The Vault and the Garden
description: Two metaphors for personal knowledge management — the vault (secure, complete, static) and the garden (living, partial, growing). You probably need both.
content:
There are two dominant metaphors for a personal knowledge system: the **vault** and the **garden**.

The vault is where you keep things safe. It's complete, organized, indexed. You put things in, you retrieve them, nothing changes. A vault is a storage unit. Its job is fidelity — what you put in is exactly what you get out.

The garden is where things grow. It's partial, messy, seasonal. You plant things, they change, some die, some cross-pollinate. A garden is a living system. Its job is transformation — what you put in is not what you get out.

> [!abstract] The two systems
> - **Vault**: archive, reference, source of truth. Search-oriented. Complete.
> - **Garden**: working memory, thinking tool, draft space. Browse-oriented. Partial.

Most people need both. The vault holds your receipts, your contracts, your reference material — the things you need to find exactly as they were. The garden holds your thinking — the things you're working on, the connections you're testing, the ideas that aren't finished yet.

The mistake is confusing them. If you treat your garden like a vault — if every note has to be polished, complete, correctly filed — you'll never plant anything. The garden's value comes from its messiness, its half-formed thoughts, its links that don't quite make sense yet.

> [!warning]
> Don't optimize your garden for retrieval before you've optimized it for growth. A clean, empty garden is just a vault with worse lighting.

[[Bikeshedding and the Garden|Bikeshedding]] is what happens when you treat the garden like a vault — spending all your time organizing instead of growing. The [[Compounding Knowledge|compounding]] effect only works when the notes are allowed to be imperfect, to sit next to each other, to cross-pollinate.

The vault is for the past. The garden is for the future. Keep them separate.

See [[Digital Gardens]], [[The Art of Note Taking]], [[Tags and Organization]].

title: Welcome to the Garden
description: A quick orientation — search, the graph, backlinks, tags, and how to actually wander this place.
content:
Welcome in. If you're wondering how to actually move through this place, here's a short orientation. A digital garden rewards wandering more than reading top-to-bottom, so the tools below are really just suggestions for getting delightfully lost.

## Search

Hit **⌘K** (or **Ctrl+K** on Windows/Linux) anywhere to open the command palette. Type a few letters and you'll see matching notes — titles, aliases, and tags all surface here. It's the fastest way to jump to a thought you half-remember.

## The graph

Every note here is connected to others through [[Wikilinks and Backlinks]]. The graph view renders those connections as a small constellation of nodes. It's not just decoration — it's a map of how ideas depend on each other. Dense clusters usually mean I've been thinking hard about something.

## Backlinks

At the bottom of every note you'll find a **backlinks** section: every other note that links *to* the one you're reading. Backlinks are the soul of a garden. They turn a one-way citation into a conversation. See [[Digital Gardens]] for why this matters.

## Tags and the explorer

Notes carry tags like `#essay` or `#reference/organization` — browse them in the tag index, or use the file explorer in the sidebar to skim everything at once. [[Tags and Organization]] goes deeper on how I nest and use them.

## A note on drafts

Not every note is published. Some are still cooking privately. If you can see it, it's meant to be read — though "meant to be read" and "finished" are different things. The full reference for formatting magic lives at [[Callouts Reference]].

Go wander.

title: Wikilinks and Backlinks
description: How wikilinks work in Obsidian-style markdown — aliases, headings, and the backlinks that make notes talk to each other.
content:
Wikilinks are the connective tissue of a digital garden. Instead of pointing at a URL, you point at the *name* of another note, and the system resolves it for you. It sounds small, but it changes how you write: you stop worrying about where things "live" and start linking the way you think.

## Basic syntax

The simplest form wraps a note's title in double brackets:

```
I was reading about [[Digital Gardens]] and it changed how I think.
```

The target matches the note's `title` in frontmatter, or any of its `aliases`. So if a note is titled "On Thinking" but aliased as "Thinking", both `[[On Thinking]]` and `[[Thinking]]` resolve to the same place.

## Aliases — link with different text

You can show different text than the target's title using a pipe:

```
The garden metaphor [[Digital Gardens|is older than you think]].
```

This renders as "is older than you think" but points to the Digital Gardens note. Useful for prose flow.

## Heading and block links

Link to a specific section with a hash:

```
See [[Digital Gardens#The stream vs. the garden]] for the contrast.
```

Block references (`[[Note#^abc123]]`) point at an individual paragraph, but I use them sparingly — headings are usually enough.

## Backlinks

Every link is bidirectional in practice. If note A links to note B, then B's page shows A in its **backlinks** section automatically. You don't author backlinks; they emerge from the graph. This is what turns a collection of files into a conversation — see [[Embeds and Transclusions]] for how to pull content *into* a note rather than just linking out.

> [!example] Try it
> This very note is linked from [[Digital Gardens]], [[Welcome to the Garden]], and [[Tags and Organization]]. Scroll to the bottom of those pages and you'll find me in their backlinks.

## When links break

If you rename a note, ideally every link to it updates too. In practice, I keep titles stable and lean on `aliases` for variants. A broken wikilink is a useful signal — it means a thought is dangling, waiting for the note it wants to become.

title: Zettelkasten Method
description: Niklas Luhmann's note-taking system — atomic notes, unique IDs, dense links. The grammar a digital garden runs on.
content:
The Zettelkasten is a note-taking method developed by the German sociologist Niklas Luhmann, who used it to write something like seventy books and four hundred articles over his career. The system is deceptively simple: write small, link densely, give every note an ID. Its power comes from what those constraints do to your thinking.

## Three principles

**Atomicity.** One idea per note. Not one topic — one *idea*. If a note tries to do two things, split it. The reason is that atomic notes can be recombined infinitely; bloated notes can only be read in the order you wrote them. An atomic note titled "the stream vs. the garden" can join a future essay you haven't imagined yet. A six-paragraph note titled "stuff about gardens" cannot. ^atomicity

**Unique IDs.** Every note gets a stable identifier. Luhmann used hierarchical numbers (`21/3d7a5`); in a digital garden the filename or title does the job. The point is permanence: a link from 2019 should still resolve in 2030. This is why I'm cautious about renaming notes — see [[Wikilinks and Backlinks]].

**Linking.** Notes connect to other notes explicitly. Links are how a Zettelkasten becomes more than a pile. When you add a note, you ask: what does this disagree with, extend, exemplify? Over time, clusters form on their own.

> [!tip] The note as a conversation
> A good Zettelkasten note doesn't just record a fact — it takes a position. Write it as if you're explaining it to a future self who will push back. That friction is what makes the note useful later.

## What it isn't

The Zettelkasten is not a filing system. You don't sort notes into folders by topic; you let links do the organizing. It's also not a journal — notes should be self-contained, not tied to the day you wrote them. And it's not a quote collection; capturing a quote without your own gloss is usually a waste, because you won't remember why you saved it.

## My checklist for a new note

- [x] Give it a precise, claim-shaped title
- [x] Write the idea in my own words
- [ ] Link it to at least two existing notes
- [ ] Add a source if it came from somewhere
- [ ] Re-read it in a week and revise

The Zettelkasten is the discipline layer that makes a garden like [[Digital Gardens]] navigable. Pair it with [[Building a Second Brain]] for the upstream capture habit, and with [[The Art of Note Taking]] for the softer philosophy of why any of this is worth doing.
